fuzzy_prd:
We need a binary encoding and decoding library for communicating with a relational database over its binary wire protocol. On the encoding side: given a typed value — integer, float, text, boolean, or null — produce the exact byte sequence the protocol expects. Integers go out in network byte order at a fixed width based on their type. Floating-point uses the standard 32-bit representation. Null produces zero bytes. For arrays, there's a structured header followed by each element with its size framed before it. Some values need to be self-describing frames that include both the type identifier and the size before the payload — handle framing the same way the parameter-binding layer handles typed nulls, where the type identifier travels alongside the value. On the decoding side: given wire bytes and the expected type, check that the incoming type code is compatible, then extract the value. Report three distinct error categories: wrong type identifier, unexpected null when the field cannot be null, and malformed payload when the byte count doesn't match the type. For sending parameterized queries, the library should assemble the per-parameter metadata: type identifier, format code (always binary for this driver), byte length, and the bytes themselves. Typed nulls send length=-1 and no payload. Finally, the system also needs a bidirectional conversion for compact alphanumeric status codes that encode error numbers in a base-36 scheme.

One small follow-up from the team questions: for the PostgreSQL type ids, please use the exact OID values bool=16, bytea=17, name=19, int8=20, int2=21, int4=23, text=25, text-array=1009, int4-array=1007, and float4 OID=700. Those are the ones we expect to flow through serialize, frame, deserialize, and bind_meta, so the same exact mapping should be used end to end rather than having different assumptions in different paths.

Also, on the array shape, the binary header should be treated as 4 x 32-bit big-endian words: (1) dimension count, (2) data-offset/flags word (0), (3) element type OID, then per dimension: (4) dimension length, (5) lower-bound word (0). After that, each element is just 32-bit big-endian size + payload bytes. That’s the structure we want for both text-array=1009 and int4-array=1007 cases.

And just to make the parameter side completely explicit: parameters are ALWAYS sent in binary format. The format code is always 1 (binary). This applies to all parameter types including text and nulls. So even when the value is text, and even for typed nulls with length=-1 and no payload, bind_meta should still mark them as binary.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: template<typename T> std::vector<uint8_t> serialize(const T& value)

trigger_keywords: how is a typed null serialized as parameter
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A typed null parameter: oid=<type's oid>, format=1, length=-1, value=<null>. An untyped null: oid=0, format=1, length=-1, value=<null>.

Three error categories: 'oid_type_mismatch' when incoming OID is not acceptable for target type; 'unexpected_null' when a null is received for a non-nullable target (null scalar or null element in non-nullable array); 'invalid_data' for malformed payloads (wrong byte count for fixed-width types, or array with more than one dimension).

base36 uses digits 0-9 then letters. Decoding is case-insensitive. Encoding outputs uppercase letters A-Z, most-significant digit first, with no leading zeros. Example: 'HV001' decodes to 29999809 and encodes back to 'HV001'.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: handle framing the same way the parameter-binding layer handles typed nulls, where the type identifier travels alongside the value
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 2 framed serialization — the frame layout is: 32-bit big-endian OID, then 32-bit big-endian payload size, then payload bytes. This mirrors how bind_meta reports oid + length + value for parameters.

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific information about that constraint. Please check the start.md specification and the golden reference implementation for details.

feature1_1_scalar_serialization.json.description: Serialize a single scalar value into its PostgreSQL binary wire representation. The request names the wire data type and the value; the output reports the number of bytes produced and those bytes as space-separated uppercase two-digit hexadecimal. Signed integers are encoded in big-endian (network) byte order using a fixed width determined by the type (1, 2, 4, or 8 bytes). A 32-bit floating point value is encoded as its IEEE-754 big-endian bit pattern. Text is stored verbatim as its raw UTF-8 bytes with no length prefix and no terminator. A null value produces no bytes at all.

feature1_2_array_serialization.json.description: Serialize a one-dimensional array of values into the PostgreSQL binary array wire format. The request names the element wire type and lists the element values; the output reports the total byte count and the produced bytes as space-separated uppercase hexadecimal. The encoding begins with a fixed header in big-endian 32-bit words: the number of dimensions, a data-offset/flags word, and the element type's numeric type identifier; then, per dimension, the dimension length and a lower-bound word; then each element is written as a 32-bit big-endian size followed by that element's own binary representation.

feature2_framed_serialization.json.description: Serialize a value as a full wire frame, the layout used when an item must carry its own type information (for example as an element of a composite). The frame is the value's numeric type identifier as a 32-bit big-endian word, followed by the value's payload size as a 32-bit big-endian word, followed by the raw payload bytes. The output reports the decoded type identifier, the payload size, and the payload bytes as space-separated uppercase hexadecimal. A raw byte string is framed as its bytes unchanged; a short identifier name is framed as its raw text bytes.

feature3_1_scalar_deserialization.json.description: Deserialize a single scalar value from PostgreSQL binary wire bytes back into a typed value. The request supplies the incoming column's numeric type identifier, the target wire type to decode into, and the raw bytes; the output reports the decoded value. Decoding succeeds only when the incoming type identifier is compatible with the target type. Big-endian integers of width 2, 4, and 8 become their numeric value; a single boolean byte becomes true or false; text bytes become the literal string; an identifier name becomes its string; a raw byte string is reported back as space-separated uppercase hexadecimal.

feature3_2_array_deserialization.json.description: Deserialize a one-dimensional array from PostgreSQL binary array bytes into a sequence of elements. The request supplies the array's numeric type identifier, the element decoding mode, and the raw bytes laid out as the binary array header (dimension count, offset/flags word, element type identifier, then per-dimension length and lower bound) followed by each element as a 32-bit big-endian size and payload. The output reports the element count and then each element on its own indexed line. When the element mode tolerates missing values, a per-element size of -1 marks that element as absent and it is rendered as a null marker. An array whose dimension count is zero yields no elements.

feature3_3_deserialization_errors.json.description: Report normalized, language-neutral error categories when deserialization cannot proceed. The request is shaped like a normal deserialize/deserialize_array request but describes a faulty situation; instead of a value, the output is a single error line naming the failure category. The categories are: oid_type_mismatch when the incoming type identifier is not acceptable for the requested target type (including an array whose declared element type identifier is wrong); unexpected_null when a null is received for a target that cannot represent null (a null value for a non-nullable scalar, or a null element for a non-nullable element sequence); and invalid_data for malformed payloads such as a fixed-width value whose byte length disagrees with the type, or an array declaring more than one dimension.

feature4_1_parameter_count.json.description: Build a parameterized binary query from a query text and a list of bound parameters, then report how many parameters were bound and the query text that will be sent. The output is the parameter count followed by the query text exactly as provided. The count equals the number of supplied parameters regardless of their individual types; the text is passed through unchanged.

feature4_2_parameter_metadata.json.description: Build a parameterized binary query carrying a single bound parameter and report the per-parameter wire metadata that the protocol requires for that parameter: its numeric type identifier, its wire format code, its serialized byte length, and its serialized bytes. Parameters are always sent in binary, so the format code is always 1. A present value reports its type's numeric identifier, the byte length of its binary form, and those bytes as space-separated uppercase hexadecimal. A typed null value reports the underlying type's identifier, a length of -1, and a null marker for the value. An untyped null reports a type identifier of 0, a length of -1, and a null marker. An array-typed value reports the array's own numeric type identifier.

cases.expected_output:
params_count=1
text=
oid=25
format=1
length=11
value=73 74 64 3A 3A 73 74 72 69 6E 67

params_count=1
text=
oid=23
format=1
length=-1
value=<null>

feature5_status_code_base36.json.description: Convert between a compact alphanumeric status code and its integer encoding using base-36. Decoding reads a status code string and interprets it as a base-36 number (digits 0-9 then letters), case-insensitively, producing the integer value. Encoding takes an integer and produces the canonical base-36 string using digits 0-9 and uppercase letters A-Z, most-significant digit first, with no leading zeros.
